data.title: MAGA media cheers Quinn Hughes for praising U.S. after game-winning goal
data.description:
Minnesota Wild defenseman Quinn Hughes emerged as an overtime hero for Team USA on Wednesday, with a goal to lift the U.S. over Sweden en route to the men’s hockey semifinals at the Winter Olympics.

Hughes’s comments celebrating his home country are earning him praise from many across the conservative media ecosystem.

“I love the U.S., and it’s the greatest country in the world. So, I’m happy to represent here with these guys, and it was really special,” Hughes said after the game.

The comments were met with adulation from a wide array of political pundits, some of whom had decried other U.S. athletes competing in Milano-Cortina who had spoken less glowingly about the United States.

“Quinn Hughes scores winning OT goal says he loves representing the US because it’s the greatest country in the world. Finally, an Olympic athlete who loves the country,” wrote the right-wing sports and politics pundit Clay Travis on social media.

Fox News host Laura Ingraham, in a social media post of her own, called Hughes “a total superstar patriot!”

Trump ally and political commentator Monica Crowley praised Hughes for expressing “pure love for America,” on social media, writing “this is how it’s done.”

“Hockey Players >>>>> Freestyle Skiers,” wrote Grace Curley, who hosts a show on the conservative streamer Rumble. “… and it ain’t even close.”

The praise of Hughes stands in contrast to an onslaught of criticism many on the right, including President Trump, have leveled against other U.S. athletes who have expressed discomfort representing the U.S. given the policies of the Trump administration.

Freestyle skier Hunter Hess sparked a firestorm last week when he said he had “mixed feelings” about competing for the U.S., while figure skater Amber Glenn earned pushback from critics over what she said is the current climate for LGBTQ people under the Trump administration.

The U.S. hockey team will face off with Slovakia on Friday for a chance to meet in Sunday’s gold medal game against heavy favorite Canada, which plays Finland in its semifinal.

A potential U.S. vs. Canada final has been widely buzzed about since the beginning of the tournament and comes against a backdrop of increasing tensions between the two countries on issues like trade and the broader North American economy.

data.title: Live updates: Trump heads to Georgia as DHS shutdown drags on; former Prince Andrew arrested
data.description:
President Trump is on his way to Georgia, where he will deliver remarks about the economy later Thursday. 

His visit to the battleground state comes as Capitol Hill remains at a standstill on negotiations to fund the Department of Homeland Security (DHS). Funding lapsed for the department over the weekend.

The White House has taken aim at Democrats, slamming the party for not making concessions amid the impasse over tactics used by Immigration and Customs Enforcement to carry out Trump’s deportation agenda. However, specific details about the discussion have not surfaced, with lawmakers and aides pointing to the relative quiet as a sign that serious talks are taking place.

Earlier Thursday, dozens of world leaders gathered in Washington for the president’s inaugural Board of Peace meeting. 

The board, which was launched by Trump last month, aims to oversee the rehabilitation of the Gaza Strip amid a fragile Israel-Hamas ceasefire and after the war’s end. Several countries, including many U.S. allies, declined to participate over concerns of the board’s framework as well as member countries, including Russia and Belarus.

Meanwhile, the Jeffrey Epstein saga has intensified with the arrest of former Prince Andrew on suspicion of misconduct while in public office after the release of millions of pages of documents in late January.

The National Governors Association’s annual meeting will also begin Thursday as Trump digs in on his criticism of some Democratic leaders ahead of the 2026 midterm elections.

Follow live.

data.title: Trump jokes he almost fired Rubio over Munich speech
data.description:
President Trump joked Thursday that he nearly “fired” Secretary of State Marco Rubio for his well-received speech last week at the Munich Security Conference.

During the first meeting of his “Board of Peace” to rebuild the Gaza strip, the president said Rubio “really did yourself proud” in Munich. 

“In fact, so proud that I almost terminated his [employment], because they were saying, ‘Why can’t Trump do this?’ I do, but I say it differently,” Trump remarked sarcastically, with Rubio in attendance alongside U.S. officials and world leaders at the U.S. Institute for Peace.  

“But Marco, don’t do any better than you did, please. Because if you do, you’re out of here,” the president added, to laughs from the crowd.

On Saturday, Rubio addressed a multitude of issues during a more than 20-minute speech in Germany. The secretary of state criticized European allies and past U.S. administrations for embracing “mass migration,” clean energy investments and global trade without consideration of the impacts at home. 

The secretary of state said the “euphoria” of the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989 “led us to a dangerous delusion: that we had entered, quote, ‘the end of history;’ that every nation would now be a liberal democracy; that the ties formed by trade and by commerce alone would now replace nationhood; that the rules-based global order — an overused term — would now replace the national interest; and that we would now live in a world without borders where everyone became a citizen of the world.”

“This was a foolish idea that ignored both human nature and it ignored the lessons of over 5,000 years of recorded human history,” Rubio added. “And it has cost us dearly.”

But America’s top diplomat also extended an olive branch to Europe, asking allies to assist the U.S. in correcting those mistakes of the past.

“Under President Trump, the United States of America will once again take on the task of renewal and restoration, driven by a vision of a future as proud, as sovereign, and as vital as our civilization’s past,” he remarked. “And while we are prepared, if necessary, to do this alone, it is our preference and it is our hope to do this together with you, our friends here in Europe. 

“For the United States and Europe, we belong together.”

The speech was met with a standing ovation, which contrasted with Vice President Vance’s frosty reception at last year’s Munich Security Conference. At the time, the vice president criticized European countries for cracking down on free speech and embracing mass migration.

Trump, though, praised his vice president Thursday, saying the “acclaim J.D. gets is great.”

data.title: Ingraham 'standing up' for Crockett in FCC, CBS, Colbert battle
data.description:
Rep. Jasmine Crockett (D-Texas) has found an unlikely supporter in Fox News host Laura Ingraham amid the controversy over a late-night talk show interview with fellow Democratic Texas Senate candidate James Talarico that was allegedly blocked from airing by the network.

Ingraham had Federal Communications Commission (FCC) Chair Brendan Carr on her show “The Ingraham Angle” on Wednesday to discuss what she called the “fake hoax of censorship” that had emerged in recent days after “Late Show” host Stephen Colbert slammed his network, CBS, over the controversy.

CBS has denied Colbert’s claims, writing in a statement that it “provided legal guidance that the broadcast could trigger the FCC equal-time rule” for other candidates, including Crockett, and “presented options for how the equal time for other candidates could be fulfilled.”

Crockett indicated during an interview on MS NOW earlier Wednesday that she had received a phone call in which she was told CBS could go ahead with Talarico’s interview if she was offered equal time

“I did not get a request from the Colbert show to go on,” Crockett said, while emphasizing that she had been on the show “multiple times” in the past.

Ingraham asked Carr to respond to the comments, which he started to, when she quickly jumped in.

“Yes, I’m standing up for Jasmine Crockett. If everyone is wondering out there. They’re all wondering why. Because, you know, you can kind of see what is going on,” she quipped, referencing Talarico’s reported $2.5 million fundraising haul in the first 24 hours after his appearance was pulled.

“Was this perhaps their plan all along?” Ingraham asked.

“Of course,” Carr replied. “He raised a lot of money, he got a lot of clicks, he wanted a leg up on his political opposition in the Democratic primary, and he knew, he knew that the legacy media would run with the story.”

The interview with Talarico, who previously bashed CBS’s decision as an attempt at censorship, was posted to YouTube, where it has racked up 7.5 million views as of Thursday.

Crockett quipped on Wednesday that publishing the interview online likely gave her primary rival a “boost,” but said she had no issue with the choice.

“There were a number of options that could be put on the table. And frankly, you know, the Late Show decided that this was the option, and I think that it was a good strategy,” she said at the time.

The fight has centered around the FCC’s “equal time” rule, which requires broadcast stations to provide equal opportunities to opposing political candidates but has historically exempted talk shows. Carr is reportedly eyeing changes that would remove those exemptions.

Talarico, a Texas state representative, is facing off against Crockett in the Democratic primary to unseat incumbent Sen. John Cornyn (R).

data.title: Mace lauds former Prince Andrew's arrest: 'The powerful spent years believing they were untouchable'
data.description:
Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.), one of the House Republicans who pushed for the public release of files related to late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, applauded the arrest of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor — formerly known as Prince Andrew — on Thursday.

“If you’re watching a former prince get arrested today, remember: four Republicans refused to flinch, refused to fold, and forced the Epstein files into the light. Courage has consequences. So does corruption. The powerful spent years believing they were untouchable,” Mace, who is running for governor of South Carolina, said in a statement. 

“Today, the world is watching them learn otherwise,” she added. 

Mace, along with Reps. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.), Lauren Boebert (R-Colo.) and now former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.), joined all House Democrats in backing a discharge petition last year to force a vote on legislation requiring the Department of Justice (DOJ) to release the Epstein files.

President Trump had been opposed to the effort initially, before reversing course and urging House Republicans to support the legislation, dubbed the Epstein Files Transparency Act. It passed the House 427-1, was unanimously approved by the Senate and was signed by Trump in November.

Documents released by the DOJ in accordance with the Epstein Files Transparency Act showed Mountbatten-Windsor sharing confidential information with Epstein during his tenure as the U.K.’s special representative for trade and investment, the BBC reported. The outlet noted, though, that the investigation into the former prince does not have to focus only on those revelations. Mountbatten-Windsor was reportedly arrested Thursday on suspicion of misconduct while in public office. 

One of Epstein’s victims, the late Virginia Giuffre, wrote in her posthumous memoir that Epstein and his associate Ghislaine Maxwell trafficked her to Mountbatten-Windsor, who sexually abused her. The former prince has denied wrongdoing in connection to Epstein. 

Mace in September had called for Mountbatten-Windsor to be “prosecuted for any and all potential crimes committed on U.S. soil.”

“A cold dark cell. Prince Andrew in handcuffs. This is an image sending the right message,” Mace said in a statement then. “No one is above the law, victims deserve justice and Americans deserve confidence our system works as it was intended.”

King Charles III had stripped his brother of his royal titles in October due to the Epstein controversy.

Max Rego contributed to this report.

data.title: Section 301 won't save Trump's tariffs if the Supreme Court strikes them down
data.description:
If the Supreme Court strikes down the sweeping tariffs that President Trump invoked under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act, the administration won’t retreat. It will just pivot.

Section 301 of the 1974 Trade Act will loom large in this regard. It expressly authorizes tariffs in response to “unreasonable” or “discriminatory” foreign trade practices that frustrate U.S. commerce. The administration has even said that Section 301 will be a key part of its Plan B for backfilling lost tariffs. 

But Section 301 cannot lawfully backfill for Trump’s current tariffs. That’s because rulings issued recently by the Court of International Trade and the Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit point up its Achilles heel.

Back in 2020, an American manufacturer of vinyl tiles, HTMX, went to the Court of International Trade to get a refund on many of Trump’s Section 301 tariffs against China. Trump initially imposed two rounds of tariffs over allegations that China had violated U.S. intellectual property violations and forced technology transfers, after conducting an investigation that found China’s practices caused $50 billion in damage to U.S. commerce.

When China retaliated with tariffs of its own, Trump responded with a third and fourth round of Section 301 tariffs, adding duties on $320 billion more Chinese imports. HTMX, joined by thousands of U.S. importers, argued that the new rounds of tariffs had given rise to “an unprecedented, unbounded and unlimited trade war,” and were untethered to the first and second rounds that had been limited to the initial $50 billion finding about the IP theft and forced technology transfer actions. 

The Court of International Trade did not disagree, but it said that it would “not try to unscramble this egg.” The statute says the executive can “modify” Section 301 tariffs when policies and practices tied to those actions have “increased or decreased.” 

As the court conceded, “China directly connected its retaliation to the U.S. action and to its own acts.” But it also said that the statute requires adjustments that remain tethered to the original investigation and objective. In other words, executive authority to “modify” does not erase the statute’s guardrails. 

The Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit reinforced that understanding. It insisted that Section 301 grants “broad,” but not unlimited, discretion. That word matters: “broad” is not a synonym for “boundless.” 

The appellate court focused on whether rounds three and four of the tariffs were sufficiently connected to the original Section 301 investigation of China. Their legality hinged on that nexus. Without it, and without a thorough and procedurally robust original determination, the action would not fit within the statutory framework. The court permitted Trump’s escalation, but only because it was responsive to developments in the same trade dispute. 

That reasoning will be critical if Section 301 underpins Plan B after a defeat on the ground of an international economic emergency. It means that Trump cannot replace his existing tariffs with Section 301 tariffs until an investigation is conducted that proves that “a foreign country” has engaged in unjustifiable or discriminatory practices that restrict U.S. commerce. 

The International Emergency Economic Powers Act tariffs were imposed across the board on all goods from all countries, but any effort to backfill with Section 301 tariffs will be limited to goods imported only from those countries that have been investigated and found to discriminate against U.S. commerce. The amount of the tariffs would also have to relate to the nature and amount of discrimination found, not to presidential whim.  

And it means that if other countries retaliate against any Section 301 tariffs, those tariffs can be adjusted, but this recalibration must be tied closely to the original investigation. Only “appropriate” modifications to properly established tariffs would be permitted.  

If every foreign countermeasure opens the door to new tariffs, then the word “modify” becomes a license for perpetual expansion. But the Court of International Trade has already underscored that Section 301 must remain within the statute’s temporal and substantive boundaries. And the Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit has made clear that judicial review is not ceremonial  — courts must examine whether the executive has stayed within the lane Congress marked.

That lane is narrower than it might appear at first blush. Section 301 is structured enforcement authority. It is not a general-purpose industrial policy statute. It requires findings. It requires a process. And any remedies must be directed at eliminating the identified burden on U.S. exports. 

If the International Emergency Economic Powers Act tariffs fall, the administration may attempt to stretch Section 301 to fill the gap. But the judiciary has already made it clear that the word “modify” does not mean “rewrite” tariffs, and that the process matters. 

If Congress one day decides to reassert its tariff authority, it, too, can start by explaining that the word “modify” is not a license to wage an all-out trade war.

Marc L. Busch is the Karl F. Landegger Professor of International Business Diplomacy at the Walsh School of Foreign Service, Georgetown University. Jennifer Hillman is a professor from practice at the Georgetown University Law Center.

data.title: Sanders pushes California wealth tax: 'Enough is enough'
data.description:
Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) on Tuesday joined a rally in Los Angeles to push for California’s controversial wealth tax, which has been opposed by Gov. Gavin Newsom (D).

“Enough is enough,” Sanders said, according to The Associated Press. “The billionaire class cannot have it all. This nation belongs to all of us.”

The senator touted the Billionaire Tax Act, which would charge the state’s 200 billionaires with a one-time five-percent tax on their net worth to help fund health care programs for middle-class and low-income Californians.

“For these people, enough is never enough,” Sanders told a rally of nearly 2,000 people, according to the Los Angeles Times. 

“They are dedicated to accumulating more and more wealth and power … no matter how many low-income and working-class people will die because they no longer have health insurance.”

The wealth tax has divided Newsom and the progressive wing of California’s Democratic Party.

“This will be defeated — there’s no question in my mind,” Newsom said early last month in an interview with the New York Times.

“I’ll do what I have to do to protect the state,” he added.

Brian Brokaw, a longtime Newsom adviser who is leading a political committee opposing the tax, argued the legislation will not fix issues of affordability around health care and education.

“In fact, they would be made worse,” he said, according to The Associated Press.

However, Sanders said the measure will help lift up thousands of low-income Californians. 

He argued that the rich believe they have a “divine right” to rule and are “no longer subject” to democratic governance, specifically calling out Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg and Google co-founder Sergey Brin.

“These billionaires are going to learn that we are still living in a democratic society where the people have some power,” the Vermont lawmaker said.

The measure has also divided the candidates running to replace Newsom, with San Jose Mayor Matt Mahan (D) joining GOP candidates Chad Bianco and Steve Hilton in opposition.

“We need ideas that are sound, not just political proposals that sound good,” Mahan said of the tax, according to the Los Angeles Times.

“The only winners in this proposal are the workers and taxpayers of Florida and Texas, who will take our jobs and benefit from the capital and tax revenue California is losing.”

The proposal has also received support from the Service Employees International Union-United Healthcare Workers West, one of the state’s largest unions.

It needs 874,641 signatures before Californians can vote for or against it. Polling conducted last month by the Mellman Group showed that out of 800 likely voters, 48 percent said they supported the proposal.

data.title: Trump says US committing $10 billion to Board of Peace
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday said the U.S. is committing $10 billion to the Board of Peace, the forum that was created to reconstruct the Gaza Strip but that the president wants to see broadened to tackle other foreign conflicts.

Trump made the pledge during the first meeting of the Board of Peace, held in Washington D.C.

“When you look at that compared to the cost of war, that’s two weeks of fighting, it’s a very small number. It sounds like a lot, but it’s a very small number,” he said.

The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment on where the funds were being drawn from. 

The United Nations Security Council gave the Board of Peace a mandate as part of its approval of the Trump administration’s 20-point plan for peace in Gaza.

But Trump is projecting a broader mission for the Board and invited dozens of countries to sign up as founding members with a $1 billion pledge for lifetime membership. It’s still unclear whether Trump’s vision for a broader Board of Peace will take away from the focus on Gaza, or whether all money pledged to the board will go to Gaza. 

Trump said $7 billion has been raised for a relief package for Gaza, provided by Kazakhstan, Azerbaijan, UAE, Morocco, Bahrain, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Uzbekistan and Kuwait.

Money for Gaza will go to a dedicated fund housed at the World Bank, called the Gaza Reconstruction and Development Fund, said President of the World Bank Ajay Banga. 

The Board of Peace — chaired by Trump, who appears to have the ultimate decision making power — directs where the money is dispersed. 

“This fund is ready to receive the donations that he spoke about just a little while ago,” Banga said at the event. 

Banga said that the Board of Peace is responsible for ensuring “that the right kind of financial legal and oversight mechanisms are being put in place,” while the World Bank is expected to help with transparency. 

“We’ve even assigned a financial controller from the World Bank on a secondary basis to the board of peace to ensure that we can try and help build the best standards in this process,” he said.

data.title: Trump touts decline in US trade deficit despite meager decrease
data.description:
President Trump on Wednesday touted the decline in U.S. trade deficit ahead of official data showing a small reduction in the country’s overall balance of trade.

In a Wednesday night post on Truth Social, the president claimed credit for reducing the trade deficit — the difference between the value of U.S. imports and exports — through tariffs he imposed last year.

Trump claimed to have cut the trade deficit by 78 percent in the Truth Social post, which came less than 12 hours before the Census Bureau released the official figures.

“THE UNITED STATES TRADE DEFICIT HAS BEEN REDUCED BY 78% BECAUSE OF THE TARIFFS BEING CHARGED TO OTHER COMPANIES AND COUNTRIES,” the president claimed.

But the Census Bureau data released Thursday showed the trade deficit falling by only 0.2 percent in 2025, from $903.5 billion in 2024 to $901.5 billion last year.

During his second term, Trump’s tariff policy has rattled markets around the globe, increased economic uncertainty and strained relationships with historic U.S. allies like Canada and the European Union.

Trump has also been dealing with anxieties about the economy from the American public as the country approaches the 2026 midterms.

Last week, six House Republicans came together with Democrats to pass a resolution to roll back Trump’s tariffs on Canada. The move by the six Republicans marked a notable defeat for Trump and Republican House leadership.

The vote, 219-211, came in the wake of a House GOP revolt against leadership due to the administration’s handling of trade policy.

Trump’s lead White House economic adviser also recently suggested that a group of Federal Reserve researchers should face punishment over a paper looking into the costs of the president’s tariffs. 

National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett, in a recent interview, criticized a new paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York economists. According to the economists, consumers paid for 90 percent of the costs of Trump’s import taxes.

Updated Feb. 19 at 10:56 a.m. EST

data.title: Midsized firms' tariff payments tripled: Analysis
data.description:
A new analysis from the JPMorgan Chase Institute found that tariff payments by midsized U.S. firms roughly tripled in 2025. 

The analysis, published Thursday, shows that midsized firms’ tariff payments began increasing starting in April, the month that President Trump unveiled his sweeping “Liberation Day” levies on numerous trading partners. Tariff payments by midsized firms steadily rose from May through November, before declining slightly in December, the analysis found. 

The institute categorized midsized firms as those with revenue between $10 million and $1 billion or with between 50 and 499 workers. The analysis found that over half of such firms conduct transactions with international partners, with over 10 percent doing business with 10 or more foreign countries.

In imposing tariffs upon returning to office, Trump said his policies would contribute to reshored U.S. manufacturing and help correct the country’s expanding trade deficit. The Census Bureau and Bureau of Economic Analysis reported Thursday that the goods and services deficit decreased $2.1 billion from 2024 to 2025.

Researchers from the New York Federal Reserve, meanwhile, released findings last week that the average tariff rate rose from 2.6 to 13 percent last year. The New York Fed also found that the share of Chinese goods imported by the U.S. has fallen dramatically since 2017, with firms importing more goods from the likes of Mexico and Vietnam, among other countries.

White House Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett said Wednesday the Fed researchers should be punished for publishing the paper, which he called “highly partisan.”

“It’s, I think, the worst paper I’ve ever seen in the history of the Federal Reserve system,” Hassett told CNBC. “The people associated with this paper should presumably be disciplined.”

The Supreme Court is also set to rule on whether the president has the authority to impose such widespread tariffs before its term ends this summer. Trump has cited the International Emergency Economic Powers Act, which allows the president to regulate economic transactions in the event of a national emergency, in imposing the import taxes.
